title: Infantile Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis
chief_complaint: My baby is vomiting forcefully after every feed. The vomit shoots across the room! He's always hungry and wants to feed again right after vomiting. He's not gaining weight. The vomit is not green or bloody.

physical_exam.General: Alert, hungry infant. Sunken fontanelle (dehydration). Dry mucous membranes. Poor skin turgor.
physical_exam.Abdomen: Visible gastric peristaltic waves from left to right across epigastrium after feeding (pathognomonic). Palpable 'olive' mass in epigastrium (hypertrophied pylorus - firm, mobile, 1-2cm).
physical_exam.Growth: Weight 3.2kg (birth weight 3.5kg - weight loss in newborn period).

hidden_data.abdominal_us: Pyloric muscle thickness 4.5mm (normal <3mm). Pyloric channel length 18mm (normal <14mm). Elongated pylorus. No passage of gastric contents through pylorus (dynamic study).
hidden_data.cmp: Na 128, K+ 2.8, Cl 82, HCO3 36, pH 7.50 (hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis - pathognomonic for pyloric stenosis). BUN 35, Cr 1.0 (dehydration).

key_learning_points: IHPS = hypertrophy of pyloric muscle → gastric outlet obstruction. Most common surgical cause of vomiting in infants. Incidence 1:500 live births. Male:Female 4:1. Classic presentation at 3-6 weeks of age

Presentation: Non-bilious projectile vomiting (pylorus proximal to ampulla of Vater - bile never enters stomach), Hungry after vomiting (want to re-feed), Weight loss/failure to thrive, Visible peristaltic waves, Palpable olive mass (pathognomonic). Vomiting is NEVER bilious (bilious = malrotation/volvulus until proven otherwise)

Lab: Hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. Loss of HCl from vomiting → loss of H+ and Cl- → metabolic alkalosis. Kidneys exchange K+ for H+ to correct alkalosis → hypokalemia. Paradoxical aciduria (acidic urine despite systemic alkalosis)

Diagnosis: Ultrasound (pyloric thickness >3mm, length >14mm). Upper GI series if US equivocal (elongated pylorus, string sign - thin stream of contrast through narrowed pylorus, shoulder sign). Do NOT operate on abnormal labs - must correct dehydration and electrolyte abnormalities first (medical emergency, NOT surgical emergency)

Pre-operative resuscitation: IV fluids (D5½NS + 20mEq/L KCl at 1.5× maintenance), correct hypokalemia and alkalosis over 24-48h. Surgery ONLY when: Cl >100, K+ >3.5, HCO3 <30, good UOP. Surgery: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy (laparoscopic or open). Longitudinal incision of serosa and pyloric muscle, leave mucosa intact. Feed 4-6h post-op. Prognosis: Excellent, curative
